subject: Physics
q:
Two identical circular wires of radius 20 cm and carrying current 2A are placed in perpendicular planes as shown in figure. The net magnetic field at the centre of the circular wires is________×10-8T.

(Take π=3.14)
question:
Two identical circular wires of radius 20 cm and carrying current 2A are placed in perpendicular planes as shown in figure. The net magnetic field at the centre of the circular wires is________×10-8T.

(Take π=3.14)
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic field B at the centre of a current carrying circular loop is given by

B= μ0i2R   ...(1)

where, μ0 is the permeability of free space, i is the current and R is the radius of the loop.

BV, BH are the magnetic field due to the vertical and the horizontal loop, which by magnitude are equal.

The resultant magnetic field Bnet at the centre is, then, given by

Bnet =B2+B2=2B=2×4π×10-7×22×0.2

=628×10-8 T

subject: Physics
q: A charge particle is moving in a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. If it has an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be
question: A charge particle is moving in a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. If it has an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be
solution:
The magnetic force on a moving charged particle is given by F→=qv→×B→. Therefore, we know that the magnetic force is perpendicular to the magnetic field. Therefore, the resulting acceleration will also be perpendicular to the magnetic field. As the scalar product of two mutually perpendicular vectors is zero,

⇒a→·B→=0 ⇒αi^-4j^·2i^+3j^=0

⇒2α-12=0⇒α=6

Statement (II) : Ampere's circuital law does not depend on Biot-Savart's law.

question:

options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution: Conceptual.

q: A solenoid of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1200</mn></math> turns is wound uniformly in a single layer on a glass tube <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in diameter. The magnetic intensity at the center of the solenoid when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows through it is:
question: A solenoid of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1200</mn></math> turns is wound uniformly in a single layer on a glass tube <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in diameter. The magnetic intensity at the center of the solenoid when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows through it is:
solution:
Given, 

Number of turns per metre n=12002

 l= 2 m, d=0.2 m and I=2 A

Magnetic field at centre inside the solenoid is given by B=μ0nI, where, μ0 is the permeability constant.

Magnetic intensity is related to magnetic field by 

H=Bμ0=μ0nIμ0=nI=12002(2)

⇒H=1.2×103 A m-1

If there is magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> the minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> for which the particle will not hit the screen is :

If there is magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> the minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> for which the particle will not hit the screen is :
solution:
The radius of circular path in given magnetic field is

r=d=mvqB0

so v=qB0dm

subject: Physics
q:
A long cylindrical conductor with large cross section carries an electric current distributed uniformly over its cross-section. Magnetic field due to this current is :
A. maximum at either ends of the conductor and minimum at the midpoint
B. maximum at the axis of the conductor
C. minimum at the surface of the conductor
D. minimum at the axis of the conductor
E. same at all points in the cross-section of the conductor
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
A long cylindrical conductor with large cross section carries an electric current distributed uniformly over its cross-section. Magnetic field due to this current is :
A. maximum at either ends of the conductor and minimum at the midpoint
B. maximum at the axis of the conductor
C. minimum at the surface of the conductor
D. minimum at the axis of the conductor
E. same at all points in the cross-section of the conductor
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
For a long cylindrical conductor with uniform current distribution, apply Ampere's law to find the magnetic field.
At distance $r$ from the axis (inside the conductor, $r < R$): $B(r) = \frac{\mu_0 I r}{2\pi R^2}$ (proportional to $r$)
At the axis ($r = 0$): $B = 0$ (minimum)
At the surface ($r = R$): $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi R}$ (maximum)
Analyzing the statements:
A. Maximum at ends and minimum at midpoint - FALSE. This describes behavior along the axis, but the magnetic field depends on radial distance, not axial position.
B. Maximum at the axis of the conductor - FALSE. The field is zero at the axis.
C. Minimum at the surface of the conductor - FALSE. The field is maximum at the surface.
D. Minimum at the axis of the conductor - TRUE. The field is zero at $r = 0$, making it the minimum.
E. Same at all points in the cross-section - FALSE. The field varies from 0 at the axis to maximum at the surface.
Only D is correct.

A rectangular loop (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>d</mi></math>) of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. Out of the following different orientations which one corresponds to stable equilibrium?
question: A uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.3</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is along the positive <math> <mtext>Z</mtext> </math> -direction. A rectangular loop (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>d</mi></math>) of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. Out of the following different orientations which one corresponds to stable equilibrium?
solution:
U=-M→·B→=-MBcosθ

For stable equilibrium potential energy should be minimum and τ=0

Umin=-MB    when θ=0°

⇒cos⁡θ = 1

⇒M→ and B→  are on same direction.

subject: Physics
q: A particle having the same charge as of electron moves in a circular path of radius 0.5 cm under the influence of a magnetic field of 0.5 T. If an electric field of 100 V/m makes it to move in a straight path, then the mass of the particle is (Given charge of electron =1.6×10-19C )
question: A particle having the same charge as of electron moves in a circular path of radius 0.5 cm under the influence of a magnetic field of 0.5 T. If an electric field of 100 V/m makes it to move in a straight path, then the mass of the particle is (Given charge of electron =1.6×10-19C )
solution:
Force to due to magnetic field

F=evB=mv2R

with electric field also, net force is zero.

∴eE=evB

⇒E=vB

E=eBRm. B

⇒m=eB2RE=1.6×10-19×0.52 . 0.5×10-2100 kg

=2.0×10-24 kg

subject: Physics
q: A long straight wire of radius ' $a$ ' caries a steady current $\mathrm{i}$. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\frac{a}{2}$ and $2 a$ is
question: A long straight wire of radius ' $a$ ' caries a steady current $\mathrm{i}$. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\frac{a}{2}$ and $2 a$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{B} 2 \pi \frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}=\mu_0 \frac{\mathrm{i}}{\pi \mathrm{a}^2}\left(\frac{\pi \mathrm{a}^2}{4}\right)$
$\mathrm{B}_1=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}} \quad \ldots$ (i)
$\mathrm{B}_2 2 \pi(2 \mathrm{a})=\mu_0 \mathrm{i}$
$\mathrm{B}_2=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}} \quad \dots (ii)$
$\frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}=1$

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field at the centre of a wire loop formed by two semicircular wires of radii R1=2π m and R2=4π m carrying current I=4 A as per figure given below is α×10-7 T. The value of α is _____. (Centre O is common for all segments)
question: The magnetic field at the centre of a wire loop formed by two semicircular wires of radii R1=2π m and R2=4π m carrying current I=4 A as per figure given below is α×10-7 T. The value of α is _____. (Centre O is common for all segments)
solution:
From the figure, it is evident that the magnetic field at O due to the straight portion of the loop is zero, only the contributions from the circular parts survive.

Also, the direction of the magnetic field due to the circular loops can be determined by the right-hand corkscrew rule.

Hence, the net magnetic field at O can be calculated as follows:

B=μ0i2R2π2π⊗+μ0i2R1π2π⊗=μ0i4R2+μ0i4R1⊗=4π×10-7×44×4π+4π×10-7×44×2π⊗ T=3×10-7⊗ T

Hence, α=3.

subject: Physics
q: An insulated wire is wound so that it forms a flat coil with $N = 200$ turns. The radius of the innermost turn is $r_1 = 3$ cm, and of the outermost turn $r_2 = 6$ cm. If $20$ mA current flows in it then the magnetic moment will be $\alpha \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
question: An insulated wire is wound so that it forms a flat coil with $N = 200$ turns. The radius of the innermost turn is $r_1 = 3$ cm, and of the outermost turn $r_2 = 6$ cm. If $20$ mA current flows in it then the magnetic moment will be $\alpha \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
solution:
Let the number of turns per unit radial width be $n = \dfrac{N}{r_2 - r_1}$.

Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$. The number of turns in this element is $dN = n \, dr$.

The magnetic moment of this elemental ring is $dM = I \, dN \, A = I \left( \dfrac{N}{r_2 - r_1} \, dr \right) (\pi r^2)$.

Integrating from $r_1$ to $r_2$ to find the total magnetic moment $M$:

$M = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{I \pi N}{r_2 - r_1} r^2 \, dr = \dfrac{I \pi N}{3(r_2 - r_1)} (r_2^3 - r_1^3)$

Substituting the given values: $I = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $N = 200$, $r_1 = 0.03$ m, and $r_2 = 0.06$ m:

$M = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times \pi \times 200}{3(0.06 - 0.03)} \left( (0.06)^3 - (0.03)^3 \right)$

$M = \dfrac{4\pi}{0.09} (216 \times 10^{-6} - 27 \times 10^{-6})$

$M = \dfrac{4\pi}{0.09} \times 189 \times 10^{-6} = 4\pi \times 2100 \times 10^{-6}$

$M = 84\pi \times 10^{-4} \approx 84 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} = 263.76 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.64 \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$, we get $\alpha = 2.64$.

Answer: $2.64$

question: A coaxial cable consists of an inner wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> surrounded by an outer shell of inner and outer radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> respectively. solution:
B1×2πx=μ0I0(x)2a2

B2×2πx=μ0I0

B1B2=x2a2

μ0i4πRsin⁡90°-sin45°⨂+μ0i2R⨀+ μ0i4πR(sin45°+sin90°)⨀
-μ0i4πR1-12+μ0i2R+μ0i4πR12+1⨀
=μ0i4πR-1+12+2π+12+1⨀=μ0i4πR2+2π⨀=μ0i2πR12+π⨀

subject: Physics
q: An electron is projected with uniform velocity along the axis inside a current carrying long solenoid. Then :
question: An electron is projected with uniform velocity along the axis inside a current carrying long solenoid. Then :
options: the electron will continue to move with uniform velocity along the axis of the solenoid.
the electron will be accelerated along the axis.
the electron path will be circular about the axis.
the electron will experience a force at $45^{\circ}$ to the axis and execute a helical path.
solution: Since $\vec{v} \| \vec{B}$ so force on electron due to magnetic field is zero. So it will move along axis with uniform velocity.

solution:
Since force on a point charge by magnetic field is always perpendicular to v→F→=qv→×B→

∴ Work by magnetic force on the point charge is zero.

q: A proton, an electron, and a Helium nucleus, have the same energy. They are in circular orbits in a plane due to magnetic field perpendicular to the plane. Let <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></msub></math> be their respective radii, then,
question: A proton, an electron, and a Helium nucleus, have the same energy. They are in circular orbits in a plane due to magnetic field perpendicular to the plane. Let <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></msub></math> be their respective radii, then,
solution:
We know, r=mvqB∵ K.E.=12mv2
 =2mK.E.qB
∴ K.E. is same for all particle and magnetic field B is also some.
r∝mq
For proton: rp∝mpe
For electron: re∝mee
For Helium nuclei: rHe∝4mp2e=mpe

subject: Physics
q:
The electric current in a circular coil of 2 turns produces a magnetic induction B1 at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of 5 turns and the same current produces a magnetic induction B2 at its centre.
The ratio of B2B1 is:
question:
The electric current in a circular coil of 2 turns produces a magnetic induction B1 at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of 5 turns and the same current produces a magnetic induction B2 at its centre.
The ratio of B2B1 is:
solution:
Magnetic field on the centre of a circular current coil is given by B=Nμ0i2R, here, N is number of turns, i is current and R is radius of loop.

Now, for, N=2, magnetic field, B1=N1μ0i2R1

For N2=5

Radius of coil =R2=N1×R1 N2

Magnetic field, B2=N2μ0i2R2

Thus, the ratio is B2 B1=N2 N1R1R2=N2 N1×N2 N1

B2 B1=254

subject: Physics
q: Two long conductors, separated by a distance $\mathrm{d}$ carry current $\mathrm{I}_1$ and $\mathrm{I}_2$ in the same direction. They exert a force $F$ on each other. Now the current in one of them increased to two times and its direction reversed. The distance is also increased to $3 \mathrm{~d}$. The new value of the force between them is
question: Two long conductors, separated by a distance $\mathrm{d}$ carry current $\mathrm{I}_1$ and $\mathrm{I}_2$ in the same direction. They exert a force $F$ on each other. Now the current in one of them increased to two times and its direction reversed. The distance is also increased to $3 \mathrm{~d}$. The new value of the force between them is
solution:
Force between two long conductor carrying current
$$
F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2}{d} \ell
$$
According to question
$$
F^{\prime}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{\left(-2 I_1\right)\left(I_2\right)}{d} \ell
$$
From equation (i) and (ii), $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}=-\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~F}$.

q: An electron with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> enters between parallel plates of a capacitor at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> with the plates. It leaves the plates at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Then the ratio of kinetic energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be :
question: An electron with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> enters between parallel plates of a capacitor at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> with the plates. It leaves the plates at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Then the ratio of kinetic energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be :
solution:
velocity along the plate will not change.

∴ v1cosα=v2cosβ

K1 K2⇒v12v22=cos2βcos2α

subject: Physics
q: Two identical wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, each of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, carry the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> . Wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is bent into a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is bent to form a square of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> are the values of magnetic field at the centres of the circle and square respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: Two identical wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, each of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, carry the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> . Wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is bent into a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is bent to form a square of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> are the values of magnetic field at the centres of the circle and square respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
As length of both the wires is same, 2πR=4a

⇒aR=2π4

⇒aR=π2

BA=μ0i2R and 

BB=μ0iπa22

Therefore, ratio will be BABB=μ0i2R×πa22 μ0i

=πa42R=π42π2=π282

subject: Physics
q:
A tightly wound long solenoid carries a current of 1.5 A . An electron is executing uniform circular motion inside the solenoid with a time period of 75 ns . The number of turns per metre in the solenoid is $\ldots\ldots$ -.
[Take mass of electron $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$, charge of electron $\left|\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$,
$\left.\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{~A}^2}, 1 \mathrm{~ns}=10^{-9} \mathrm{~s}\right]$
question:
A tightly wound long solenoid carries a current of 1.5 A . An electron is executing uniform circular motion inside the solenoid with a time period of 75 ns . The number of turns per metre in the solenoid is $\ldots\ldots$ -.
[Take mass of electron $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$, charge of electron $\left|\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{e}}\right|=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$,
$\left.\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{~A}^2}, 1 \mathrm{~ns}=10^{-9} \mathrm{~s}\right]$
solution:
Since time period of a revolving charge is $\frac{2 \pi m}{q B}$
Where $\mathrm{B}=$ magnetic field
due to a solenoid $=\mu_0 \mathrm{nI}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{q}\left(\mu_0 \mathrm{nI}\right)} \\
& 75 \times 10^{-9}=\frac{(2 \pi)\left(9 \times 10^{-31}\right)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \mathrm{n} \times 1.5} \\
& \mathrm{~N}=250
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled asReason R.
Assertion A:. If oxygen ion $\left(\mathrm{O}^{-2}\right)$ and Hydrogen ion $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$enter normal to the magnetic field with equal momentum, then the path of $\mathrm{O}^{-2}$ ion has a smaller curvature than that of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$.
Reason R : A proton with same linear momentum as an electron will form a path of smaller radius of curvature on entering a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled asReason R.
Assertion A:. If oxygen ion $\left(\mathrm{O}^{-2}\right)$ and Hydrogen ion $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$enter normal to the magnetic field with equal momentum, then the path of $\mathrm{O}^{-2}$ ion has a smaller curvature than that of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$.
Reason R : A proton with same linear momentum as an electron will form a path of smaller radius of curvature on entering a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true but $\mathrm{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$.
Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true and $\mathrm{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & r=\frac{m v}{q B}=\frac{p}{q B} \\ & r \propto \frac{1}{q}\end{aligned}$
Assertion is true reason is false

subject: Physics
q: The current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows in a square loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed in air. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is _________.
question: The current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows in a square loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed in air. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is _________.
solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of the square loop is given by,

B=4μ0I4πdsin45°+sin45°, where d is perpendicular distance of the centre to one of its sides.

⇒B=4×μ0×54π×1212+12

=4×10-7×5×2×2

=402×10-7 T

subject: Physics
q: Magnitude of magnetic field (in SI units) at the centre of a hexagonal shape coil of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn></math> turns and carrying current<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>  (Ampere) in units of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>I</mi></mrow><mi>π</mi></mfrac></math> is :
question: Magnitude of magnetic field (in SI units) at the centre of a hexagonal shape coil of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn></math> turns and carrying current<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>  (Ampere) in units of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>I</mi></mrow><mi>π</mi></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
The magnetic field due to the current-carrying finite length wire is given by

B=μ0I4πRsinθ1+sinθ2

Here for hexagonal shape coil, θ1=θ2=30°.

So, the net magnetic field at the centre will be B=50×6×μ0i4π10100cos 30°sin 30°+sin 30°

B=2×75×10μ0i3π12+12

B=15003μ0iπ   =5003μ0iπ

subject: Physics
q: A proton, a deuteron and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle are moving with same momentum in a uniform magnetic field. The ratio of magnetic forces acting on them ______ is and their speed is ______ in the ratio.
question: A proton, a deuteron and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle are moving with same momentum in a uniform magnetic field. The ratio of magnetic forces acting on them ______ is and their speed is ______ in the ratio.
solution:
F=qv→×B→=qmP→×B→

⇒F∝qm

thus,
 F1:F2:F3=q1 m1:q2 m2:q3 m3=emp:e2mp:2e4mp=11:12:24=2:1:1

Now for speed calculation, P=constant⇒v∝1 m

thus v1:v2:v3=1 mp:12mp:14mp=11:12:14=4:2:1.

subject: Physics
q: A current carrying rectangular loop PQRS is made of uniform wire. The length PR=QS=5 cm and PQ=RS=100 cm. If ammeter current reading changes from I to 2I, the ratio of magnetic forces per unit length on the wire PQ due to wire RS in the two cases respectively fPQI:fPQ2I is:
question: A current carrying rectangular loop PQRS is made of uniform wire. The length PR=QS=5 cm and PQ=RS=100 cm. If ammeter current reading changes from I to 2I, the ratio of magnetic forces per unit length on the wire PQ due to wire RS in the two cases respectively fPQI:fPQ2I is:
solution:
Force acting per unit length between two parallel wires is given by, f=μ0I1I22πd.

Clearly, f∝I1I2

Therefore, fPQI:fPQ2I=I×I2I×2I=1:4

The charged particle is shifted from the origin to the point $\mathrm{P}(x=1 ; y=1)$ along a straight path. The magnitude of the total work done is :

subject: Physics
q:
The magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of an equilateral triangular loop of side 1 m which is carrying a current of 10 A is:

[Take  μ0=4π×10-7 N A-2 ]
question:
The magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of an equilateral triangular loop of side 1 m which is carrying a current of 10 A is:

[Take  μ0=4π×10-7 N A-2 ]
solution:
The perpendicular distance of centroid from any side, 123 m
Magnetic field due to one side.
B1=µoI4πr[cosθ1+cosθ2]
=μ0(10)4πr123cos30°+cos30°
=53π μ0 32+32=15μ0π
Net magnetic field B=3B1=45πμ0=45π×4π×10-7
=180×10-7 T
=18 μT

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>314</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> carrying current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is bent to form a circle. The magnetic moment of the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>]
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>314</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> carrying current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is bent to form a circle. The magnetic moment of the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>]
solution:
Length of the wire l when bent becomes circumference of the loop, whose radius is given by R=l2π=3141002×3.14=12 m

Magnetic moment of the coil is M=IA

=14×πR2

=14×3.14×14

=10.99≈11.00 A-m2

subject: Physics
q: An element $\Delta l=\Delta x \hat{i}$ is placed at the origin and carries a large current $I=10 \mathrm{~A}$. The magnetic field on the $y$-axis at a distance of $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ from the elements $\Delta x$ of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ length is:
question: An element $\Delta l=\Delta x \hat{i}$ is placed at the origin and carries a large current $I=10 \mathrm{~A}$. The magnetic field on the $y$-axis at a distance of $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ from the elements $\Delta x$ of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ length is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dB}} & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi} \frac{(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d} l} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})}{\mathrm{r}^3}(\text { Tesla) } \\ & =\frac{10^{-7} \times 10 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{100}\right)(+\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3}=4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~T}(+\hat{\mathrm{k}})\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A circular coil of radius $2$ cm and $125$ turns carries a current of $1$ A. The coil is placed in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $0.4$ T. The axis of the coil makes an angle of $30°$ with the direction of the magnetic field. The torque acting on the coil is $\alpha \times 10^{-4}$ N.m. The value of $\alpha$ is ______.
($\pi=3.14$)
question:
A circular coil of radius $2$ cm and $125$ turns carries a current of $1$ A. The coil is placed in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $0.4$ T. The axis of the coil makes an angle of $30°$ with the direction of the magnetic field. The torque acting on the coil is $\alpha \times 10^{-4}$ N.m. The value of $\alpha$ is ______.
($\pi=3.14$)
solution:
The magnetic moment of the coil is given by $M = NIA$.

The torque acting on the coil is $\tau = MB \sin\theta = NIAB \sin\theta$.

Substituting the given values:

$\tau = 125 \times 1 \times (\pi \times (0.02)^2) \times 0.4 \times \sin 30^\circ$

$\tau = 125 \times 1 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 \times 0.5$

$\tau = 100\pi \times 10^{-4}$

$\tau = 100 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} = 314 \times 10^{-4}$ N.m

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-4}$ N.m, we get $\alpha = 314$.

Answer: $314$

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an <math><mi>α</mi></math>- particle (with their masses in the ratio of <math><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and charges in the ratio of <math><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> ) are accelerated from rest through a potential difference <math><mi>V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If a uniform magnetic field <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is set up perpendicular to their velocities, the ratio of the radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></msub></math> of the circular paths described by them will be:
question: A proton and an <math><mi>α</mi></math>- particle (with their masses in the ratio of <math><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and charges in the ratio of <math><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> ) are accelerated from rest through a potential difference <math><mi>V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If a uniform magnetic field <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is set up perpendicular to their velocities, the ratio of the radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi></mrow></msub></math> of the circular paths described by them will be:
solution:
The kinetic energy of a charge q of mass m in potential is given by,

12mv2=qV

mv=2mqV

Then, the radius of the charge will be,

r=2mqVqB

⇒r∝mq

Hence, the ratio of radius of proton to α-particle,

rprα=14×21=12.

subject: Physics
q: Two short magnetic dipoles m1 and m2 each having magnetic moment of 1 A m2 are placed at point O and P respectively. The distance between OP is 1 m. The torque experienced by the magnetic dipole m2 due to the presence of m1 is ________×10-7N m
question: Two short magnetic dipoles m1 and m2 each having magnetic moment of 1 A m2 are placed at point O and P respectively. The distance between OP is 1 m. The torque experienced by the magnetic dipole m2 due to the presence of m1 is ________×10-7N m
solution:
τ→=M→2×B→1

τ=M2B1sin90∘=1×μ04πM(1)3×1=10−7N m
